data.narrative: The student completed Module 3 by turning an intermittent redirect failure into a repeatable concurrency reproduction, then using the same proof discipline to catch a quieter silent data corruption bug. They demonstrated strong understanding of check-then-act races, lost updates, and why a reproduction script must assert state integrity, not just HTTP success.

dimension_scores.observation: Chose a specific, testable concurrency hypothesis and explained why data and time-of-day were not the main variables.
dimension_scores.evidence: Identified concurrency as the variable because a known-good /pop123 link worked sequentially but failed under 10 simultaneous requests.

dimension_scores.observation: Used server log timestamps and database state together to identify silent timestamp corruption.
dimension_scores.evidence: Compared the actual last request timestamp from logs, 2026-06-01 14:00:03.952, against database last_accessed_at, 2026-06-01 14:00:03.110.

dimension_scores.observation: Built a minimal synthetic concurrency reproduction and used it repeatedly to verify both the original failure and the final fix.
dimension_scores.evidence: Ran 10 concurrent requests against http://127.0.0.1:3000/pop123, first reproducing 1 redirect and 9 HTTP 500s, then verifying five batches of 10 requests with zero 500s, exact +10 count increments, and matching max timestamps.

dimension_scores.observation: Explained the incident mechanics clearly and separated simulated proof from live proof when challenged.
dimension_scores.evidence: Acknowledged the earlier target URL issue, corrected the endpoint, and later framed the final proof as a five-run concurrency matrix.

dimension_scores.observation: Traced past the HTTP 500 symptom to two distinct race patterns: check-then-act insertion and non-monotonic timestamp overwrite.
dimension_scores.evidence: Identified last_accessed_at = EXCLUDED.last_accessed_at as unsafe because commit order could overwrite a newer timestamp with an older request timestamp, then proposed GREATEST for monotonic updates.

dimension_scores.observation: Captured the operational lesson in a concise, memorable way and proposed a regression-test improvement.
dimension_scores.evidence: Wrote that loud errors get fixed, but silent corruption breaks data integrity slowly while pretending everything is fine, and recommended a self-contained integration test with setup, SQL assertions, and cleanup.

strengths: Excellent reproduction discipline: the student isolated concurrency as the single changed variable and used exact status counts, database rows, and logs as proof.
Strong root-cause analysis: the student understood both the loud unique-constraint race and the silent lost-update timestamp race, then chose an atomic monotonic database fix.
Good engineering integrity: the student corrected earlier incomplete proof and distinguished live execution from structural simulation before advancing.
data.recommendation: In Module 4, keep this same evidence standard, but add tool-driven measurement: use profiling output to prove where time is spent instead of relying on code reading alone.

public_evidence.student_action: Created and ran a synthetic concurrency test against the redirect endpoint to reproduce intermittent HTTP 500 errors deterministically.
public_evidence.technical_detail: The script sends 10 concurrent GET requests to http://127.0.0.1:3000/pop123 and summarizes HTTP status counts.
public_evidence.proof: A live run produced 1 HTTP 302 redirect and 9 HTTP 500 errors with duplicate key violations on analytics_link_id_bucket_unique.
public_evidence.ability: Builds deterministic reproductions for intermittent production bugs
public_evidence.reviewer_value: Shows the student can turn a flaky incident into repeatable proof that can guide a fix.

public_evidence.student_action: Fixed concurrent analytics updates by combining count and timestamp changes into one atomic upsert with monotonic timestamp selection.
public_evidence.technical_detail: The ON CONFLICT update increments click_count and sets last_accessed_at using GREATEST(analytics_tracking.last_accessed_at, EXCLUDED.last_accessed_at).
public_evidence.proof: Five batches of 10 concurrent requests completed with zero 500s, exact +10 count increments per batch, and database last_accessed_at matching the max server-log timestamp each time.
public_evidence.ability: Fixes race conditions and verifies data integrity under concurrency
public_evidence.reviewer_value: Shows the student can address both visible failures and silent data corruption with database-level atomicity.

concepts_demonstrated.evidence: Changed only concurrency while keeping endpoint and data constant.
Explained how concurrent requests could both see no analytics row and try to insert the same unique key.
Explained how an older request timestamp could overwrite a newer one when updates commit out of order.
Used ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE to handle count and timestamp updates in one database operation.
Recognized that clean HTTP responses and correct counts can still hide wrong last_accessed_at data.

teaching_approaches.detail: Used a concrete 10-request concurrency script and before/after status-count evidence.
